MECHANISMS UNDERLYING THE PLANNING FALLACY
When predicting task completion, we should derive our predictions from both
(i) singular information – aspects of the particular task at hand, and
(ii) distributional information – the distribution of outcomes for similar tasks, e.g.,
your own past experiences (personal distributional information) or past
experiences of others (populational distributional information).
Factors that prevent us from generating an accurate prediction:
• The inside–outside model (neglect of past experiences + narrow focus on plans
for successful task completion)
• Conjunctive-events and disjunctive-events bias
7

The Inside–Outside Model
The inside–outside model of planning fallacy claims that people rely far more on
singular information (inside view) than on distributional information (outside
view) when estimating the completion time / resources needed of a task.
People tend to generate their predictions by considering the unique features of
the task at hand and constructing a scenario of their successful task completion,
i.e., to develop a series of steps that will lead from the beginning to a successful
conclusion of the project. Doing so results in reduced attention to past experience
and future impediments.
Let’s examine:
1. Why people fail to incorporate past experience (distributional information) in
their completion estimates?
2. Why the resulting focus on singular information is dominated by plans for
successful task completion (vs. obstacles)?
8

Despite the predictive value of past experience (distributional information),
we neglect them while forming prediction. Why might this be so?
1. The forward nature of prediction: The act of prediction, by its very nature,
elicits a focus on the future rather than on the past; a future orientation may
prevent individuals from looking backward in time.
2. Attributional processes that diminish the relevance of past failures to the
present: To the extent that people perceive a previous failure to be caused
by external, unstable, and specific factors, they need not connect the past
failure to future occasions.
9

Consider the following study (in which think-aloud procedure was used):
• First, participants (UG students) described a school project that they planned to
complete in the next 2 weeks and its deadline. Then were asked to (i) predict as
accurately as possible when they would finish the project, and (ii) indicate how
certain they were about the predicted time.
• Second, participants were asked to recall (i) one occasion in which they had failed
to complete a task by the time they originally predicted and a similar prediction
failure experienced by a friend or acquaintance, then (ii) explain why each of the
two tasks was not finished by the expected time.
• Participants were interviewed 1 week after the project deadline to record the
actual completion time.

• Results:
Predicted vs. actual completion times:
On average, participants reported they were 83.5% certain that they would
complete the project in the predicted time. But only 43.6% of the participants
finished their projects within the predicted time.
Verbal protocols on prediction:
A 7-category system was developed to characterize the content of participants’
verbal protocols as they generated their prediction. For each participant, the
proportion of responses assigned to each category was calculated. (Please see next
slide for information on those categories.)
Consistent with the inside–outside model, participants (i) focused more on future
scenarios than past experiences, and (ii) thought more about plans for future
progress than potential impediments.
11

finish the project half an hour before it's due."
12

Verbal protocols on attribution:
A set of 7-point scales was developed to rate the attributions along 3 dimensions:
The extent to which the cause (i) implicated other people and circumstances or the
person themselves (external vs. internal), (ii) was temporary or persistent
(transitory vs. stable), and (iii) was specific to the particular project or relevant to
many different activities (specific vs. global).
Comparing to attributions offered for others’ prediction failures, participants’
attributions for their own prediction failures were more external, transitory, and
specific (e.g., “my delay was an accident” vs. “my friend failed to get the work done
as predicted because he is a procrastinator”), i.e., participants made use of
attributional mechanisms to discount the relevance of past failures.
Buehler, R., Griffin, D., & Ross, M. (1994). Exploring the ‘‘planning fallacy’’: Why people underestimate their task completion
times. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 67, 366–381. Study 3.
13

Why is the inside view (singular information) dominated by plans for
successful task completion (vs. possible obstacles or interruptions)?
Motivated reasoning is a form of reasoning in which people construct arguments
in a biased fashion to arrive at a preferred conclusion (i.e., a conclusion that
people are motivated to draw).
In the domain of planning, our goal / motivation to finish tasks quickly enhances
the accessibility of information that is consistent with the desired goal, this leads
to a greater focus on successful future plans and a reduced focus on potential
obstacles.
14

Consider a study on the role of motivated reasoning in planning fallacy:
• Participants were asked to complete a series of word puzzle tasks:

• After the first two tasks were completed, the motivation manipulation were
introduced:
Speed incentive condition: US$2 reward for finishing 1 min faster than the
previous tasks and US$4 reward for finishing 2 mins faster.
No incentive condition: Simply being told to finish as quickly as possible.
• Participants then (i) predicted how long it would take to complete the 3rd task,
(ii) wrote down thoughts that came through their minds when making the
prediction, and (iii) completed the 3rd task.
15

• Results:
Predicted vs. actual completion times:
Participants in the speed incentive (vs.
no incentive) condition predicted shorter
completion times, i.e., suffered more from
planning fallacy.
Verbal protocols on prediction:
A 4-category system was developed to
characterize the content of participants’ verbal
protocols.
- Using past: Using past tasks as the basis for
predicting their completion time (e.g., “My first
time was longer than the second, so I chose a
time half way between them”).
16

- Discounting past: Considered the past tasks but explained why the upcoming task
would be different from the previous ones (e.g., “I looked at my last time score but
decided that I would probably do better because of practice”).
- Future planning: Plans and strategies for completing the upcoming task (e.g., “Next
time I’ll look for letter E”).
- Future impediments: Problems or impediments that might be encountered with the
upcoming task (e.g., “The next set of words might be more difficult”).
Participants in the speed incentive (vs. no incentive) condition generated
(i) fewer thoughts about using the past as a basis for prediction, (ii) more thoughts
designed to deny the relevance of the past tasks, (iii) more thoughts about future
plans or strategies, and (iv) fewer thoughts about potential problems.
Buehler, R., Griffin, D., & MacDonald, H. (1997). The role of motivated reasoning in optimistic time predictions. Personality and
Social Psychology Bulletin, 23(3), 238-247. Study 2.
17

Conjunctive-events and Disjunctive-events Bias
Conjunctive-events bias: People tend to
overestimate the probability of conjunctive
events (the “AND events”).
E.g., You are trying to catch Flight 1, which Flight 1

too high (near 30%), the actual likelihood the product of the components:

18

Disjunctive-events bias: People tend to
underestimate the probability of disjunctive

19

Conjunctive-events and disjunctive-events bias offers an explanation for planning
fallacy: When constructing future scenarios of multifaceted projects, we tend to
underestimate the likelihood that our plans might go wrong.
• E.g., A building can only be completed on time if there are no delays in the
delivery of materials, no strikes, no shortages of funds during construction, no
unusual weather conditions, and so on. Although any one of these setbacks
may be unlikely, the probability of at least one of these setbacks occurring is
considerably more so.
20

DEBIASING THE PLANNING FALLACY
Discussion:
Let’s suppose you’re a programmer, and you’re developing a computer game. To
complete the game development, you have to design a mockup, complete the
main scene (play button, help button), game scene (score display, sound effects, object
movement), win scene (“you win”, fireworks animation), lose scene (“game over”, restart
button), help scene (text, back button).
Your boss asked you to estimate when the computer game will be ready so that
he can announce the product launch date accordingly.
Given the mechanisms of planning fallacy (e.g., neglect of past experiences,
narrow focus on plans for successful task completion, conjunctive-events and
disjunctive-events bias), what can you / your boss do to reduce planning fallacy
(and thus the negative consequences associated with it)?
21

Let’s examine some examples of debiasing strategies:
1. Use past distributions as the basis for prediction.
• To reduce planning fallacy, decision makers should base their prediction on
actual performance in a reference class of comparable projects (distributional
information) – Reference class forecasting.
• E.g., In a software company, programmers were significantly underestimating
the amount of time it would take them to complete various tasks, leading to
missed deadlines and burnout. Rather than trying to change the
programmers’ estimates, the company accommodated the planning fallacy by
adding “buffer time” to projects. The company looked at historical data on
work completion dates to determine how much buffer time to add to each
project, such as 20% for a minor software update or 40% for a more complex
operating system update.
22

2. Use unpacking to reveal the complexity (potential impediments) of the task.
Consider an experiment:
• Participants were given 9 unformatted text (9 definitions adapted from a
dictionary), and were asked to edit the text using MS Word so that it looked
exactly like the properly formatted version.
• Participants were asked to unpack the task into subcomponents, i.e., to list
each and every specific step that would be needed in order to complete the
formatting task such as “adding italics”, “adding punctuation”, and “capitalizing
letters”.
Kruger, J., & Evans, M. (2004). If you don't want to be late, enumerate: Unpacking reduces the planning fallacy. Journal of
Experimental Social Psychology, 40(5), 586-598. Studies 3 and 5.
23

Escalation of commitment happens when decision makers continue to dedicate
resources (e.g., time and money) to a failing course of action.

MECHANISMS UNDERLYING THE ESCALATION OF COMMITMENT
Sometimes it makes sense to persist in face of negative feedback (i.e., it’s rational):
• The expectancy explanation
Sometimes the decision to escalate our commitment is based on irrational thoughts:
• Sunk cost fallacy
• Goal substitution effect
• Self-justification theory
• Self-presentation theory

The Expectancy Explanation
The expectancy explanation: Decision makers exhibit greater escalation tendency
when the expected value of persistence (vs. withdrawal) is larger, e.g.,
 When the value of goal attainment is higher
 When the probability of goal attainment is higher

A demonstration
 Scenario 1: Imagine that you paid HK$100 for a ticket to see a horror movie.

In the previous case, the money used to purchase the movie ticket is a sunk cost –
a cost that was incurred in the past and cannot be recovered once spent.
Rationally (from standard economics sense), sunk costs should be ignored when
making decisions about future investment. All that matters should be future costs
and benefits.
In the previous case, what should you consider?
• The enjoyment you get from doing something else if you walk out of the movie
theater.
• The enjoyment (or disappointment) of watching the rest of the movie.
• Not the sunk cost!
In the context of the escalation of commitment, decision makers are unwilling to
withdraw from the failing course of action because they are unwilling to accept the
previously committed resources as a loss (closing an account “in red” is extremely
painful).

Goal Substitution Effect
Goal substitution effect: As decision makers approach project completion, they
substitute a project completion goal for their original project success goal. This
goal substitution leads to the escalation of commitment towards the project.
• For example: Consider a failing R&D project, although the original goal may have
been profit maximization, as decision makers approach project completion, the
new goal may be nothing more than completing the R&D project regardless of
financial returns.

• Results: Escalation tendency is higher in the high (vs. low) degree of completion

Self-justification Theory
Self-justification theory: Negative feedback calls the validity of the prior decision
into question. Decision makers don’t like to think that their past decision was a
mistake. To maintain their ego, they invest more to the failing course of action in
hope to turn the situation around.

Self-presentation Theory
According to self-presentation theory, people are motivated to strategically
manage the impressions others have of them (those others could include
supervisors, coworkers, followers, etc.).
In escalation situations, decision makers want to avoid any public embarrassment
in being associated with a failed project. Moreover, because of the social norm
that values decision consistency, people try to be seen as good decision makers by
persisting with their original choice.

Social norm that encourages decision consistency
“A President must, plainly, show himself to be a man made confident by the
courage of his own clear convictions. … The American people find it easy to
forgive a leader's great mistakes, but not long meanderings.”
--- Fortune Magazine (1978), in an article criticizing President Jimmy Carter
A study has shown that leaders who are consistent in their actions
are perceived to be better leaders than those who switch from one
line of behavior to another.
In order to be seen as effective leaders, decision makers exhibit the
escalation of commitment.
Staw, B.M., & Ross, J. (1980). Commitment in an experimenting society: A study of the attribution of leadership from administrative
scenarios. Journal of Applied Psychology, 65(3), 249-260.

Psychological Ownership and Confirmation Bias
• Psychological ownership: Decision makers may develop psychological ownership
with a chosen course of action, experience a need to cultivate and protect the
chosen course of action.
Psychological ownership can be developed even without personal responsibility
due to the psychological connection among individuals.
• Confirmation bias: With an aim to protect the chosen course of action (due to
psychological ownership), decision makers are likely to notice evidence that
supports the prior decision, and ignore negative feedback.
Being unaware of the negative feedback, decision makers persist with the prior
decision.
20

The case of Kodak digital camera
Kodak Brownie camera Kodak Kodachrome Kodak invented
(1900) color film (1935) digital camera (1975)
• George Eastman (Kodak’s founder) aims to make photography “as convenient
as the pencil”. In 1900, the Brownie camera brought photography within
financial reach of the general public. In 1935, Kodak launched the Kodachrome
color film and became the market leader in film business. In 1975, Kodak
invented the world’s first digital camera; but it held back from manufacturing
digital cameras for the mass market, for the fear of killing its film business.
• Many Kodak employees are proud of the success of the film business, i.e.,
psychologically connected to Kodak’s film business.
21

• In 1981, a team inside Kodak assessed the threat that would be posed by digital
technology. The report concluded that:
“The quality of prints from electronic images will not be generally acceptable
to consumers as replacement for prints based on the science of photography
[i.e., film].”
“The consumer’s desire to handle, display, and distribute prints cannot be
replaced by electronic display devices.”
“Electronic systems (camera and viewing input device for TV) will not be low
enough in price to have widespread appeal.”
There’s a whiff of confirmation bias in these conclusions. Kodak cannot see the
negative feedback.
Carroll, P. B., & Mui, C. (2008). Billion Dollar Lessons: What You Can Learn From the Most Inexcusable Business Failures of the Last
Twenty-five Years. New York: Portfolio.
22

DEBIASING THE ESCALATION OF COMMITMENT
Discussion:
Suppose you’re a bank loan officer, and you’ve authorized a loan ($1M) to a small
business 2 years ago. The repayment deadline is approaching, but the small
business has not repaid the loan yet, and it is now applying for extra loan (another
$1M) from you.
Given the mechanisms of irrational escalation of commitment (e.g., sunk cost
fallacy, self-justification and self-presentation theories, psychological ownership
and confirmation bias), what can you / the bank do to reduce irrational
commitment to problem loans?
23

Let’s examine some examples of debiasing strategies:
1. Remove initial decision makers from subsequent decisions.
• To reduce irrational escalation, organizations could change the decision maker;
the new decision maker is not responsible for the failing course of action, thus
has a lower level of justification pressure.
• Data collected from 132 California banks over a 9-year period showed that the
turnover of bank managers led to reduced commitment to problem loans,
manifested as:
- Increase in the provisions for loan losses ($$ set aside in anticipation of
uncollectible loans)
- Increase in the write-off of bad loans (considered loans as uncollectible)
Staw, B.M., Barsade, S.G., & Koput, K.W. (1997). Escalation at the credit window: A longitudinal study of bank executives'
recognition and write-off of problem loans. Journal of Applied Psychology, 82(1), 130-142.
24

2. Use self-distancing (i.e., to take the perspective of a neutral observer).
• To reduce irrational escalation, decision makers could do
self-distancing and adopt an observer's perspective – in
their mental image they should see themselves as part of
the field of view.
By taking an observer's perspective, decision makers
disregard the emotional attachment to the past, engage
in more balanced reasoning.
• Example: Intel abandoned a once-promising line of business – memory chips:
Intel’s business had been built on memory chips. The business was
profitable until Japanese competitors came into market. In 1978, Intel
experienced a steep decline in its market share for memory. In 1981, IBM
selected Intel’s microprocessor to power the IBM personal computers.
25

During early 1980s, Intel became a company with two products: memory (which is
under threat) and microprocessor (which seems to have opportunity to grow).
There was a debate about resource allocation among these two businesses.
In 1985, after months of fruitless debate, Andy Grove (President of Intel) had an
insight during a meeting with Gordon Moore (CEO) …
I looked out the window at the Ferris Wheel of Great America amusement park
revolving in the distance, then I turned back to Gordon and I asked, “if we got kicked
out and the board brought in a new CEO, what do you think he would do?” Gordon
answered without hesitation, “He would get us out of memories.”
I stared at him, numb, then said, “Why shouldn’t you and I walk out the door, come
back in, and do it ourselves?”
Using self-distancing, executives at Intel prevented irrational escalation, exited its
original memory business to concentrate on microprocessors.
Grove, A. S. (1996). Only the Paranoid Survive. New York: Currency Doubleday.
26

v The endowment effect
Consider an experiment:
The seller condition:
• “You now own the mug, you can keep and take it home. You also have the option
of selling it and receive money for it.”
• A series of prices ranging from $0.25 to $9.25 was listed. “For each of the prices
listed below, please indicate whether you wish to (1) sell your mug and receive
that amount of money, or (2) keep your mug. After you have finished, one of the
prices listed will be selected at random and any exchanges will take place at that
price.”
The chooser condition:
• For each of the prices, participants were asked to indicate whether they wish to
receive (1) a mug, or (2) cash.
Kahneman, D., Knetsch, J.L., & Thaler, R.H. (1990). Experimental tests of the endowment effect and the Coase theorem. Journal of
Political Economy, 98(6), 1325-1348. Experiment 6.
22

Results:
• On average, sellers asked for at least $7.12 to sell the mug.
• Whereas, choosers decided to receive money rather than the mug at $3.12.
Why?
For sellers, their reference point is “having the mug”, they are facing the prospect of
losing the mug.
For choosers, their reference point is “no mug”, they are facing the prospect of
gaining the mug.
Due to loss aversion, sellers need a larger amount of $$ to compensate the loss of
the mug, compared to choosers’ valuation of the mug.
This is called the endowment effect: People place a greater value on something they
already possess than on something equivalent that they do not possess. (We value
things more when we own them.)

PROSPECT THEORY WEIGHTING FUNCTION
Prospect theory’s weighting function relates
an objective probability to people’s
subjective understanding of the probability
(called decision weight).
The weighting function has 2 properties:
1) Possibility effect: A change in probability
from 0% to a small probability can create
a huge psychological impact.
• When p is very small, w(p) > p.
2) Certainty effect: A change in probability
from a large probability to 100% can
create a huge psychological impact.
• When p is very large, w(p) < p.

Possibility Effect and Certainty Effect
Take a look on the following 4 changes: In each case, your chance of receiving
$1 million improves by 5%. Is the news equally good in each case?
0% à 5% 15% à 20%
60% à 65% 95% à 100%
• The change “0% à 5%” has a large psychological impact, because it creates
a possibility that did not exist earlier (possibility effect).
• The change “95% à 100%” has a large psychological impact, because it
eliminates an uncertainty (certainty effect).
• Certainty and possibility have similarly strong effects in the domains of
gains and losses.

In the previous case, the money used to purchase the movie ticket is a sunk cost –
a cost that was incurred in the past and cannot be recovered once spent.
Rationally (from standard economics sense), sunk costs should be ignored when
making decisions about future investment. All that matters should be future costs
and benefits.
In the previous case, what should you consider?
• The enjoyment you get from doing something else if you walk out of the movie
theater.
• The enjoyment (or disappointment) of watching the rest of the movie.
• Not the sunk cost!
In the context of the escalation of commitment, decision makers are unwilling to
withdraw from the failing course of action because they are unwilling to accept the
previously committed resources as a loss (closing an account “in red” is extremely
painful).

Goal Substitution Effect
Goal substitution effect: As decision makers approach project completion, they
substitute a project completion goal for their original project success goal. This
goal substitution leads to the escalation of commitment towards the project.
• For example: Consider a failing R&D project, although the original goal may have
been profit maximization, as decision makers approach project completion, the
new goal may be nothing more than completing the R&D project regardless of
financial returns.

Self-presentation Theory
According to self-presentation theory, people are motivated to strategically
manage the impressions others have of them (those others could include
supervisors, coworkers, followers, etc.).
In escalation situations, decision makers want to avoid any public embarrassment
in being associated with a failed project. Moreover, because of the social norm
that values decision consistency, people try to be seen as good decision makers by
persisting with their original choice.

Social norm that encourages decision consistency

A study has shown that leaders who are consistent in their actions
are perceived to be better leaders than those who switch from one

In order to be seen as effective leaders, decision makers exhibit the
escalation of commitment.

Psychological Ownership and Confirmation Bias
• Psychological ownership: Decision makers may develop psychological ownership
with a chosen course of action, experience a need to cultivate and protect the
chosen course of action.
Psychological ownership can be developed even without personal responsibility
due to the psychological connection among individuals.
• Confirmation bias: With an aim to protect the chosen course of action (due to
psychological ownership), decision makers are likely to notice evidence that
supports the prior decision, and ignore negative feedback.
Being unaware of the negative feedback, decision makers persist with the prior
decision.

as the pencil”. In 1900, the Brownie camera brought photography within
financial reach of the general public. In 1935, Kodak launched the Kodachrome
color film and became the market leader in film business. In 1975, Kodak
invented the world’s first digital camera; but it held back from manufacturing
digital cameras for the mass market, for the fear of killing its film business.
• Many Kodak employees are proud of the success of the film business, i.e.,
psychologically connected to Kodak’s film business.
21

• In 1981, a team inside Kodak assessed the threat that would be posed by digital
technology. The report concluded that:
“The quality of prints from electronic images will not be generally acceptable
to consumers as replacement for prints based on the science of photography
[i.e., film].”
“The consumer’s desire to handle, display, and distribute prints cannot be
replaced by electronic display devices.”
“Electronic systems (camera and viewing input device for TV) will not be low
enough in price to have widespread appeal.”
There’s a whiff of confirmation bias in these conclusions. Kodak cannot see the
negative feedback.
Carroll, P. B., & Mui, C. (2008). Billion Dollar Lessons: What You Can Learn From the Most Inexcusable Business Failures of the Last
Twenty-five Years. New York: Portfolio.
22

DEBIASING THE ESCALATION OF COMMITMENT
Discussion:
Suppose you’re a bank loan officer, and you’ve authorized a loan ($1M) to a small
business 2 years ago. The repayment deadline is approaching, but the small
business has not repaid the loan yet, and it is now applying for extra loan (another
$1M) from you.
Given the mechanisms of irrational escalation of commitment (e.g., sunk cost
fallacy, self-justification and self-presentation theories, psychological ownership
and confirmation bias), what can you / the bank do to reduce irrational
commitment to problem loans?
23

Let’s examine some examples of debiasing strategies:
1. Remove initial decision makers from subsequent decisions.
• To reduce irrational escalation, organizations could change the decision maker;
the new decision maker is not responsible for the failing course of action, thus
has a lower level of justification pressure.
• Data collected from 132 California banks over a 9-year period showed that the
turnover of bank managers led to reduced commitment to problem loans,
manifested as:
- Increase in the provisions for loan losses ($$ set aside in anticipation of
uncollectible loans)
- Increase in the write-off of bad loans (considered loans as uncollectible)
Staw, B.M., Barsade, S.G., & Koput, K.W. (1997). Escalation at the credit window: A longitudinal study of bank executives'
recognition and write-off of problem loans. Journal of Applied Psychology, 82(1), 130-142.
24

2. Use self-distancing (i.e., to take the perspective of a neutral observer).
• To reduce irrational escalation, decision makers could do
self-distancing and adopt an observer's perspective – in
their mental image they should see themselves as part of
the field of view.
By taking an observer's perspective, decision makers
disregard the emotional attachment to the past, engage
in more balanced reasoning.
• Example: Intel abandoned a once-promising line of business – memory chips:
Intel’s business had been built on memory chips. The business was
profitable until Japanese competitors came into market. In 1978, Intel
experienced a steep decline in its market share for memory. In 1981, IBM
selected Intel’s microprocessor to power the IBM personal computers.
25

During early 1980s, Intel became a company with two products: memory (which is
under threat) and microprocessor (which seems to have opportunity to grow).
There was a debate about resource allocation among these two businesses.
In 1985, after months of fruitless debate, Andy Grove (President of Intel) had an
insight during a meeting with Gordon Moore (CEO) …
I looked out the window at the Ferris Wheel of Great America amusement park
revolving in the distance, then I turned back to Gordon and I asked, “if we got kicked
out and the board brought in a new CEO, what do you think he would do?” Gordon
answered without hesitation, “He would get us out of memories.”
I stared at him, numb, then said, “Why shouldn’t you and I walk out the door, come
back in, and do it ourselves?”
Using self-distancing, executives at Intel prevented irrational escalation, exited its
original memory business to concentrate on microprocessors.
Grove, A. S. (1996). Only the Paranoid Survive. New York: Currency Doubleday.
26
